[[Image:Nijo Castle Karamon2.JPG|thumb|250px|A ''karamon'' at [[Nijō Castle]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''karamon''''' or '''''karakado'''''|唐門}} is a type of [[gate]] seen in [[Japanese architecture]]. It is characterized by the usage of ''[[karahafu]]'', an undulating [[bargeboard]] peculiar to Japan. ''Karamon'' are often used at the entrances of [[Japanese castle|Japanese castles]], [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], and have historically been a symbol of authority.
==History==
{{main|Karahafu}}
Although ''kara'' (唐) can be translated as meaning "China" or "[[Tang dynasty|Tang]]", this type of roof with undulating bargeboards is an invention of Japanese carpenters in the late [[Heian period]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu 唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' could also mean "noble" or "elegant", and was often added to names of objects considered grand or intricate regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the [[Heian period]] and is shown in [[Emakimono|picture scrolls]] to decorate gates, corridors, and [[palanquins]].
Initially, the ''karahafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the [[Azuchi-Momoyama period]], it became an important architectural element in the construction of a [[daimyo|daimyo's]] mansions and castles. The ''karamon'' entrance was reserved for the [[shogun]] during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments. A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82-84, 178.</ref>
''Karamon'' would later became a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80-108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''karamon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture. <ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>
==Variations== [[Image:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|130px|A ''Mukaikaramon'' at [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]]] ===Mukaikaramon===
''Mukaikaramon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''karamon'', and features two ''karahafu'' at the front and back of the gate. This type of gate may incorporate a ''karahafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon 向唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
[[Image:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|130px|A ''hirakaramon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]] ===Hirakaramon===
''Hirakaramon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''karahafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was called ''Miyukimon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
[[Image:Nikko Toshogu Gate.jpg|thumb|130px|A ''karayotsuashimon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] ===Karayotsuashimon===
''Karayotsuashimon'' (唐四脚門, lit. ''Four-legged gate'') is an ornate style of ''karamon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]].
==See also==
*[[Karahafu]] *[[Japanese architecture]] *[[Japanese castle]] *[[Buddhist temples in Japan]] *[[Shinto shrine]]
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== <references/> {{refbegin}} *Coaldrake, William. (1996). ''Architecture and Authority in Japan''. London/New York: Routledge. ISBN 041505754X. *Sarvimak Marja. (2000). ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings: Chinese and Korean Influence on Japanese Architecture''. Helsinki University of Technology, Department of Architecture. ISBN 0521369185. *Sarvimak Marja. (2003). ''[http://sjeas.skku.edu/upload/200605/05_Sarvimaki%20Marja.pdf Layouts and Layers: Spatial Arrangements in Japan and Korea]''. Sungkyun Journal of East Asian Studies, Volume 3, No. 2. Retrieved on May 30, 2009. *Parent, Mary Neighbour. (2003). ''[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System]''. {{refend}}
[[Category:Japanese architecture]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] {{japan-struct-stub}}

[[Image:Nijo Castle Karamon2.JPG|thumb|250px|A ''karamon'' at [[Nijō Castle]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''karamon''''' or '''''karakado'''''|唐門}} is a type of [[gate]] seen in [[Japanese architecture]]. It is characterized by the usage of ''[[karahafu]]'', an undulating [[bargeboard]] peculiar to Japan. ''Karamon'' are often used at the entrances of [[Japanese castle|Japanese castles]], [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], and have historically been a symbol of authority.
==History==
{{main|Karahafu}}
Although ''kara'' (唐) can be translated as meaning "China" or "[[Tang dynasty|Tang]]", this type of roof with undulating bargeboards is an invention of Japanese carpenters in the late [[Heian period]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu 唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' could also mean "noble" or "elegant", and was often added to names of objects considered grand or intricate regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the [[Heian period]] and is shown in [[Emakimono|picture scrolls]] to decorate gates, corridors, and [[palanquins]].
Initially, the ''karahafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the [[Azuchi-Momoyama period]], it became an important architectural element in the construction of a [[daimyo|daimyo's]] mansions and castles. The ''karamon'' entrance was reserved for the [[shogun]] during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments. A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82-84, 178.</ref>
''Karamon'' would later became a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80-108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''karamon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture. <ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>
==Variations== [[Image:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|130px|A ''Mukaikaramon'' at [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]]] ===Mukaikaramon===
''Mukaikaramon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''karamon'', and features two ''karahafu'' at the front and back of the gate. This type of gate may incorporate a ''karahafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon 向唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
[[Image:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|130px|A ''hirakaramon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]] ===Hirakaramon===
''Hirakaramon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''karahafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyukimon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
[[Image:Nikko Toshogu Gate.jpg|thumb|130px|A ''karayotsuashimon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] ===Karayotsuashimon===
''Karayotsuashimon'' (唐四脚門, lit. ''Four-legged gate'') is an ornate style of ''karamon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]].

''[http://sjeas.skku.edu/upload/200605/05_Sarvimaki%20Marja.pdf Layouts and Layers: Spatial Arrangements in Japan and Korea]''. Sungkyun Journal of East Asian Studies, Volume 3, No. 2. ''Mukaikaramon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''karamon'', and features two ''karahafu'' at the front and back of the gate. This type of gate may incorporate a ''karahafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon 向唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>

''Hirakaramon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''karahafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyukimon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]." JAANUS. ''Karayotsuashimon'' (唐四脚門, lit. ''Four-legged gate'') is an ornate style of ''karamon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]].

JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' could also mean "noble" or "elegant", and was often added to names of objects considered grand or intricate regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." ''Karayotsuashimon'' (唐四脚門, lit. ''Four-legged gate'') is an ornate style of ''karamon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]].
{{-}}

[[Image:Nijo Castle Karamon2.JPG|thumb|250px|A ''karamon'' at [[Nijō Castle]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''karamon''''' or '''''karakado'''''|唐門}} is a type of [[gate]] seen in [[Japanese architecture]]. It is characterized by the usage of ''[[karahafu]]'', an undulating [[bargeboard]] peculiar to Japan. ''Karamon'' are often used at the entrances of [[Japanese castle|Japanese castles]], [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], and have historically been a symbol of authority.
==History==
{{main|Karahafu}}
Although ''kara'' (唐) can be translated as meaning "China" or "[[Tang dynasty|Tang]]", this type of roof with undulating bargeboards is an invention of Japanese carpenters in the late [[Heian period]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu 唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' could also mean "noble" or "elegant", and was often added to names of objects considered grand or intricate regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the [[Heian period]] and is shown in [[Emakimono|picture scrolls]] to decorate gates, corridors, and [[palanquins]].
Initially, the ''karahafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the [[Azuchi-Momoyama period]], it became an important architectural element in the construction of a [[daimyo|daimyo's]] mansions and castles. The ''karamon'' entrance was reserved for the [[shogun]] during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments. A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82-84, 178.</ref>
''Karamon'' would later became a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80-108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''karamon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture. <ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>
==Variations==
===Mukaikaramon===
[[Image:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|130px|left|A ''Mukaikaramon'' at [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]
''Mukaikaramon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''karamon'', and features two ''karahafu'' at the front and back of the gate. This type of gate may incorporate a ''karahafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon 向唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{-}}
===Hirakaramon===
[[Image:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|130px|left|A ''hirakaramon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]
''Hirakaramon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''karahafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyukimon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{-}}
===Karayotsuashimon===
[[Image:Nikko Toshogu Gate.jpg|thumb|130px|left|''Yōmeimon'', a ''karayotsuashimon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Karayotsuashimon'' (唐四脚門, lit. ''Four-legged gate'') is an ornate style of ''karamon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]].
{{-}}

Retrieved on May 30, 2009. *Parent, Mary Neighbour. (2003). ''[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System]''. {{refend}}

JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' could also mean "noble" or "elegant", and was often added to names of objects considered grand or intricate regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." ===Hirakaramon===
[[Image:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|130px|left|A ''hirakaramon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]

This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyukimon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{-}}
===Karayotsuashimon===
[[Image:Nikko Toshogu Gate.jpg|thumb|130px|left|''Yōmeimon'', a ''karayotsuashimon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Karayotsuashimon'' (唐四脚門, lit. ''Four-legged gate'') is an ornate style of ''karamon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]].

JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' could also mean "noble" or "elegant", and was often added to names of objects considered grand or intricate regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the [[Heian period]] and is shown in [[Emakimono|picture scrolls]] to decorate gates, corridors, and [[palanquins]].
Initially, the ''karahafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the [[Azuchi-Momoyama period]], it became an important architectural element in the construction of a [[daimyo|daimyo's]] mansions and castles. The ''karamon'' entrance was reserved for the [[shogun]] during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments. A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82-84, 178.</ref>
''Karamon'' would later became a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80-108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''karamon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture. <ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>

JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{-}}

[[Image:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|130px|left|A ''hirakaramon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]
''Hirakaramon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''karahafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>

===Karayotsuashimon===
[[Image:Nikko Toshogu Gate.jpg|thumb|130px|left|''Yōmeimon'', a ''karayotsuashimon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Karayotsuashimon'' (唐四脚門, lit. ''Four-legged gate'') is an ornate style of ''karamon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]].

[[Image:Nijo Castle Karamon2.JPG|thumb|250px|A ''karamon'' at [[Nijō Castle]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''karamon''''' or '''''karakado'''''|唐門}} is a type of [[gate]] seen in [[Japanese architecture]]. It is characterized by the usage of ''[[karahafu]]'', an undulating [[bargeboard]] peculiar to Japan. ''Karamon'' are often used at the entrances of [[Japanese castle|Japanese castles]], [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], and have historically been a symbol of authority.
==History==
{{main|Karahafu}}
The [[kanji]] of ''kara'', 唐 (pronounced as [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] in Chinese), is the name of an [[Dynasties in Chinese history|imperial dynasty of China]] spans from 618 to 907, This type of roof with undulating bargeboards first appeared in Japan in the late [[Heian period]],<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu 唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> where Chinese cultural, artistic, and religious influence on Japan were at its peak.<ref>[http://search.eb.com/eb/article-9039814 Heian period], Encyclopædia Britannica.</ref> As a result, the word ''kara'' were often often prefixed to names of objects of art to connoted elegance and noble appearance at the time. <ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the [[Heian period]] and is shown in [[Emakimono|picture scrolls]] to decorate gates, corridors, and [[palanquins]].
Initially, the ''karahafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the [[Azuchi-Momoyama period]], it became an important architectural element in the construction of a [[daimyo|daimyo's]] mansions and castles. The ''karamon'' entrance was reserved for the [[shogun]] during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments. A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82-84, 178.</ref>
''Karamon'' would later became a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80-108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''karamon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture. <ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>
==Variations==
===Mukaikaramon===
[[Image:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|130px|left|A ''Mukaikaramon'' at [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]
''Mukaikaramon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''karamon'', and features two ''karahafu'' at the front and back of the gate. This type of gate may incorporate a ''karahafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon 向唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{-}}
===Hirakaramon===
[[Image:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|130px|left|A ''hirakaramon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]
''Hirakaramon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''karahafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyukimon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{-}}
===Karayotsuashimon===
[[Image:Nikko Toshogu Gate.jpg|thumb|130px|left|''Yōmeimon'', a ''karayotsuashimon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Karayotsuashimon'' (唐四脚門, lit. ''Four-legged gate'') is an ornate style of ''karamon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]].
{{-}}

JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> where Chinese cultural, artistic, and religious influence on Japan were at its peak.<ref>[http://search.eb.com/eb/article-9039814 Heian period], Encyclopædia Britannica.</ref> As a result, the word ''kara'' were often often prefixed to names of objects of art to connoted elegance and noble appearance at the time. <ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the [[Heian period]] and is shown in [[Emakimono|picture scrolls]] to decorate gates, corridors, and [[palanquins]].

===Mukaikaramon===
[[Image:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|130px|left|A ''Mukaikaramon'' at [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]
''Mukaikaramon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''karamon'', and features two ''karahafu'' at the front and back of the gate. This type of gate may incorporate a ''karahafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon 向唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{-}}
===Hirakaramon===
[[Image:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|130px|left|A ''hirakaramon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]
''Hirakaramon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''karahafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyukimon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{-}}
===Karayotsuashimon===
[[Image:Nikko Toshogu Gate.jpg|thumb|130px|left|''Yōmeimon'', a ''karayotsuashimon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Karayotsuashimon'' (唐四脚門, lit. ''Four-legged gate'') is an ornate style of ''karamon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]].
{{-}}

''Structures, Symbols and Meanings: Chinese and Korean Influence on Japanese Architecture''. Helsinki University of Technology, Department of Architecture. ISBN 0521369185. *Sarvimaki Marja. (2003). ''[http://sjeas.skku.edu/upload/200605/05_Sarvimaki%20Marja.pdf Layouts and Layers: Spatial Arrangements in Japan and Korea]''. Sungkyun Journal of East Asian Studies, Volume 3, No. 2. Retrieved on May 30, 2009. *Parent, Mary Neighbour. (2003). ''[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System]''. {{refend}}

{{main|Mon (architecture)}} [[Image:Nijo Castle Karamon2.JPG|thumb|250px|A ''karamon'' at [[Nijō Castle]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''karamon''''' or '''''karakado'''''|唐門|lit. Chinese gate}} is a type of [[gate]] seen in [[Japanese architecture]]. It is characterized by the usage of ''[[karahafu]]'', an undulating [[bargeboard]] peculiar to Japan. ''Karamon'' are often used at the entrances of [[Japanese castle|Japanese castles]], [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], and have historically been a symbol of authority.
==History==
{{main|Karahafu}}
The [[kanji]] of ''kara'', 唐 (pronounced as [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] in Chinese), is the name of an [[Dynasties in Chinese history|imperial dynasty of China]] spans from 618 to 907, This type of roof with undulating bargeboards first appeared in Japan in the late [[Heian period]],<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu 唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> where Chinese cultural, artistic, and religious influence on Japan were at its peak.<ref>[http://search.eb.com/eb/article-9039814 Heian period], Encyclopædia Britannica.</ref> As a result, the word ''kara'' were often often prefixed to names of objects of art to connoted elegance and noble appearance at the time. <ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the [[Heian period]] and is shown in [[Emakimono|picture scrolls]] to decorate gates, corridors, and [[palanquins]].
Initially, the ''karahafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the [[Azuchi-Momoyama period]], it became an important architectural element in the construction of a [[daimyo|daimyo's]] mansions and castles. The ''karamon'' entrance was reserved for the [[shogun]] during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments. A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82-84, 178.</ref>
''Karamon'' would later became a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80-108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''karamon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture. <ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>
==Variations==
===Mukaikaramon===
[[Image:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|130px|left|A ''Mukaikaramon'' at [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]
''Mukaikaramon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''karamon'', and features two ''karahafu'' at the front and back of the gate. This type of gate may incorporate a ''karahafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon 向唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{-}}
===Hirakaramon===
[[Image:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|130px|left|A ''hirakaramon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]
''Hirakaramon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''karahafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyukimon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{-}}
===Karayotsuashimon===
[[Image:Nikko Toshogu Gate.jpg|thumb|130px|left|''Yōmeimon'', a ''karayotsuashimon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Karayotsuashimon'' (唐四脚門, lit. ''Four-legged gate'') is an ornate style of ''karamon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]].
{{-}}

==References== <references/> {{refbegin}} *Coaldrake, William. (1996). ''Architecture and Authority in Japan''. London/New York: Routledge. ISBN 041505754X. *Sarvimaki Marja. (2000). ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings: Chinese and Korean Influence on Japanese Architecture''. Helsinki University of Technology, Department of Architecture. ISBN 0521369185. *Sarvimaki Marja. (2003). ''[http://sjeas.skku.edu/upload/200605/05_Sarvimaki%20Marja.pdf Layouts and Layers: Spatial Arrangements in Japan and Korea]''. Sungkyun Journal of East Asian Studies, Volume 3, No. 2. Retrieved on May 30, 2009. *Parent, Mary Neighbour. (2003). ''[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System]''. {{refend}}

{{main|Mon (architecture)}} [[Image:Nijo Castle Karamon2.JPG|thumb|250px|A ''karamon'' at [[Nijō Castle]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''karamon''''' or '''''karakado'''''|唐門|lit. Chinese gate}} is a type of [[gate]] seen in [[Japanese architecture]]. It is characterized by the usage of ''[[karahafu]]'', an undulating [[bargeboard]] peculiar to Japan. ''Karamon'' are often used at the entrances of [[Japanese castle|Japanese castles]], [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], and have historically been a symbol of authority.
==History==
{{main|Karahafu}}
Although ''kara'' (唐) can be translated as meaning "China" or "[[Tang dynasty|Tang]]", this type of roof with undulating bargeboards is first appeared during the late [[Heian period]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu 唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' could also mean "noble" or "elegant", and was often added to names of objects considered grand or intricate regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the [[Heian period]] and is shown in [[Emakimono|picture scrolls]] to decorate gates, corridors, and [[palanquins]]. The oldest existing ''karahafu'' is found at [[Hōryū-ji]] temple.<ref name=JAANUS/>
Initially, the ''karahafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the [[Azuchi-Momoyama period]], it became an important architectural element in the construction of a [[daimyo|daimyo's]] mansions and castles. The ''karamon'' entrance was reserved for the [[shogun]] during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments. A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82-84, 178.</ref>
''Karamon'' would later became a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80-108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''karamon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture. <ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>
==Variations==
===Mukaikaramon===
[[Image:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|130px|left|A ''Mukaikaramon'' at [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]
''Mukaikaramon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''karamon'', and features two ''karahafu'' at the front and back of the gate. This type of gate may incorporate a ''karahafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon 向唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{-}}
===Hirakaramon===
[[Image:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|130px|left|A ''hirakaramon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]
''Hirakaramon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''karahafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyukimon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{-}}
===Karayotsuashimon===
[[Image:Nikko Toshogu Gate.jpg|thumb|130px|left|''Yōmeimon'', a ''karayotsuashimon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Karayotsuashimon'' (唐四脚門, lit. ''Four-legged gate'') is an ornate style of ''karamon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]].
{{-}}

''[http://sjeas.skku.edu/upload/200605/05_Sarvimaki%20Marja.pdf Layouts and Layers: Spatial Arrangements in Japan and Korea]''. Sungkyun Journal of East Asian Studies, Volume 3, No. 2. Retrieved on May 30, 2009. *Parent, Mary Neighbour. (2003). ''[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System]''. {{refend}}

===Hirakaramon===
[[Image:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|130px|left|A ''hirakaramon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]
''Hirakaramon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''karahafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{-}}
===Karayotsuashimon===
[[Image:Nikko Toshogu Gate.jpg|thumb|130px|left|''Yōmeimon'', a ''karayotsuashimon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Karayotsuashimon'' (唐四脚門, lit. ''Four-legged gate'') is an ornate style of ''karamon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]].

''Structures, Symbols and Meanings: Chinese and Korean Influence on Japanese Architecture''. Helsinki University of Technology, Department of Architecture. ISBN 0521369185. *Sarvimaki Marja. (2003). ''[http://sjeas.skku.edu/upload/200605/05_Sarvimaki%20Marja.pdf Layouts and Layers: Spatial Arrangements in Japan and Korea]''. Sungkyun Journal of East Asian Studies, Volume 3, No. 2. {{main|Mon (architecture)}} [[File:Nijo Castle Karamon2.JPG|thumb|250px|A ''karamon'' at [[Nijō Castle]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''karamon''''' or '''''karakado'''''|唐門|lit. Chinese gate}} is a type of [[gate]] seen in [[Japanese architecture]]. It is characterized by the usage of ''[[karahafu]]'', an undulating [[bargeboard]] peculiar to Japan. ''Karamon'' are often used at the entrances of [[Japanese castle|Japanese castles]], [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], and have historically been a symbol of authority.
==History==
{{main|Karahafu}}
Although ''kara'' (唐) can be translated as meaning "China" or "[[Tang dynasty|Tang]]", this type of roof with undulating bargeboards first appeared during the late [[Heian period]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu 唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' could also mean "noble" or "elegant", and was often added to names of objects considered grand or intricate regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the [[Heian period]] and is shown in [[Emakimono|picture scrolls]] to decorate gates, corridors, and [[palanquins]]. The oldest existing ''karahafu'' is found at [[Hōryū-ji]] temple.<ref name=JAANUS/>
Initially, the ''karahafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the [[Azuchi-Momoyama period]], it became an important architectural element in the construction of a [[daimyo|daimyo's]] mansions and castles. The ''karamon'' entrance was reserved for the [[shogun]] during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments. A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82-84, 178.</ref>
''Karamon'' would later became a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80-108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''karamon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture. <ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>
==Variations==
===Mukaikaramon===
[[File:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|130px|left|A ''Mukaikaramon'' at [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]
''Mukaikaramon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''karamon'', and features two ''karahafu'' at the front and back of the gate. This type of gate may incorporate a ''karahafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon 向唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{-}}
===Hirakaramon===
[[File:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|130px|left|A ''hirakaramon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]
''Hirakaramon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''karahafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyukimon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{-}}
===Karayotsuashimon===
[[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|130px|left|''Yōmeimon'', a ''karayotsuashimon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Karayotsuashimon'' (唐四脚門, lit. ''Four-legged gate'') is an ornate style of ''karamon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]].
{{-}}

*[[Karahafu]] *[[Japanese architecture]] *[[Japanese Buddhist architecture]] *[[Japanese castle]] *[[Buddhist temples in Japan]] *[[Shinto shrine]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]]

==References== <references/> {{refbegin}} *Coaldrake, William. (1996). ''Architecture and Authority in Japan''. London/New York: Routledge. ISBN 0-415-05754-X. *Sarvimaki Marja. (2000). ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings: Chinese and Korean Influence on Japanese Architecture''. Helsinki University of Technology, Department of Architecture. ISBN 0-521-36918-5. *Sarvimaki Marja. (2003). ''[http://sjeas.skku.edu/upload/200605/05_Sarvimaki%20Marja.pdf Layouts and Layers: Spatial Arrangements in Japan and Korea]''. Sungkyun Journal of East Asian Studies, Volume 3, No. 2. Retrieved on May 30, 2009. *Parent, Mary Neighbour. (2003). ''[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System]''. {{refend}}

The oldest existing ''karahafu'' is found at [[Hōryū-ji]] temple.<ref name=JAANUS/>

The ''karamon'' entrance was reserved for the [[shogun]] during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments. ''Karamon'' would later became a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80-108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''karamon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture. <ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>

This type of gate may incorporate a ''karahafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon 向唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{-}}
===Hirakaramon===
[[File:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|130px|left|A ''hirakaramon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]
''Hirakaramon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''karahafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyukimon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{-}}
===Karayotsuashimon===
[[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|130px|left|''Yōmeimon'', a ''karayotsuashimon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Karayotsuashimon'' (唐四脚門, lit. ''Four-legged gate'') is an ornate style of ''karamon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]].
{{-}}

{{main|Mon (architecture)}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Nijo Castle Karamon2.JPG|thumb|250px|A ''karamon'' at [[Nijō Castle]]]]
The {{nihongo|'''''karamon''''' or '''''karakado'''''|唐門|chinese gate}} is a type of [[gate]] seen in [[Japanese architecture]]. It is characterized by the usage of ''[[karahafu]]'', an undulating [[bargeboard]] peculiar to Japan. ''Karamon'' are often used at the entrances of [[Japanese castle]]s, [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[Shinto shrine]]s, and have historically been a symbol of authority.
==History==
{{main|Karahafu}}
Although ''kara'' (唐) can be translated as meaning "China" or "[[Tang dynasty|Tang]]", this type of roof with undulating bargeboards first appeared during the late [[Heian period]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu 唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' could also mean "noble" or "elegant", and was often added to names of objects considered grand or intricate regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the [[Heian period]] and is shown in [[Emakimono|picture scrolls]] to decorate gates, corridors, and [[palanquins]]. The oldest existing ''karahafu'' is found at [[Hōryū-ji]] temple.<ref name=JAANUS/>
Initially, the ''karahafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the [[Azuchi-Momoyama period]], it became an important architectural element in the construction of a [[daimyo|daimyo's]] mansions and castles. The ''karamon'' entrance was reserved for the [[shogun]] during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments. A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82-84, 178.</ref>
''Karamon'' would later become a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80-108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''karamon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture.<ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>
==Variations==
===Mukaikaramon===
[[File:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|130px|left|A ''Mukaikaramon'' at [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]
''Mukaikaramon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''karamon'', and features two ''karahafu'' at the front and back of the gate. This type of gate may incorporate a ''karahafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon 向唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Hirakaramon===
[[File:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|130px|left|A ''hirakaramon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]
''Hirakaramon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''karahafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyukimon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Karayotsuashimon===
[[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|130px|left|''Yōmeimon'', a ''karayotsuashimon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Karayotsuashimon'' (唐四脚門, lit. ''Four-legged gate'') is an ornate style of ''karamon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]].
{{Clear}}

[[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|130px|left|''Yōmeimon'', a ''karayotsuashimon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Karayotsuashimon'' (唐四脚門, lit. ''Four-legged gate'') is an ornate style of ''karamon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]].
{{Clear}}

{{main|Mon (architecture)}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Nijo Castle Karamon2.JPG|thumb|250px|A ''karamon'' at [[Nijō Castle]]]]
The {{nihongo|'''''karamon''''' or '''''karakado'''''|唐門|chinese gate}} is a type of [[gate]] seen in [[Japanese architecture]]. It is characterized by the usage of ''[[karahafu]]'', an undulating [[bargeboard]] peculiar to Japan. ''Karamon'' are often used at the entrances of [[Japanese castle]]s, [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[Shinto shrine]]s, and have historically been a symbol of authority.
==History==
{{main|Karahafu}}
Although ''kara'' (唐) can be translated as meaning "China" or "[[Tang dynasty|Tang]]", this type of roof with undulating bargeboards first appeared during the late [[Heian period]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu 唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' could also mean "noble" or "elegant", and was often added to names of objects considered grand or intricate regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the [[Heian period]] and is shown in [[Emakimono|picture scrolls]] to decorate gates, corridors, and [[palanquins]]. The oldest existing ''karahafu'' is found at [[Hōryū-ji]] temple.<ref name=JAANUS/>
Initially, the ''karahafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the [[Azuchi–Momoyama period]], it became an important architectural element in the construction of a ''[[daimyō]]''{{'}}s mansions and castles. The ''karamon'' entrance was reserved for the ''[[shōgun]]'' during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments. A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82–84, 178.</ref>
''Karamon'' would later become a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80–108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''karamon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture.<ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>
==Variations==
===Mukaikaramon===
[[File:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|130px|left|A ''Mukaikaramon'' at [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]
''Mukaikaramon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''karamon'', and features two ''karahafu'' at the front and back of the gate. This type of gate may incorporate a ''karahafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon 向唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Hirakaramon===
[[File:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|130px|left|A ''hirakaramon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]
''Hirakaramon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''karahafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyukimon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Karayotsuashimon===
[[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|130px|left|''Yōmeimon'', a ''karayotsuashimon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Karayotsuashimon'' (唐四脚門, lit. ''Four-legged gate'') is an ornate style of ''karamon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]].
{{Clear}}

{{Clear}}
===Karayotsuashimon===
[[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|upright|left|''Yōmeimon'', a ''karayotsuashimon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Karayotsuashimon'' (唐四脚門, "Four-legged gate") is an ornate style of ''karamon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]].
{{Clear}}

revision.text:
{{main|Mon (architecture)}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Nijo Castle Karamon2.JPG|thumb|250px|A ''karamon'' at [[Nijō Castle]]]]
The {{nihongo|'''''karamon''''' or '''''karakado'''''|唐門|Chinese gate}} is a type of [[gate]] seen in [[Japanese architecture]]. It is characterized by the usage of ''[[karahafu]]'', an undulating [[bargeboard]] peculiar to Japan. ''Karamon'' are often used at the entrances of [[Japanese castle]]s, [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[Shinto shrine]]s, and have historically been a symbol of authority.
==History==
{{main|Karahafu}}
Although ''kara'' (唐) can be translated as meaning "China" or "[[Tang dynasty|Tang]]", this type of roof with undulating bargeboards first appeared during the late [[Heian period]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu 唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' could also mean "noble" or "elegant", and was often added to names of objects considered grand or intricate regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the [[Heian period]] and is shown in [[Emakimono|picture scrolls]] to decorate gates, corridors, and [[palanquins]]. The oldest existing ''karahafu'' is found at [[Hōryū-ji]] temple.<ref name=JAANUS/>
Initially, the ''karahafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the [[Azuchi–Momoyama period]], it became an important architectural element in the construction of a ''[[daimyō]]''{{'}}s mansions and castles. The ''karamon'' entrance was reserved for the ''[[shōgun]]'' during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments. A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82–84, 178.</ref>
''Karamon'' would later become a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80–108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''karamon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture.<ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>
==Variations==
===Mukaikaramon===
[[File:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|upright|left|A ''Mukaikaramon'' at [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]
''Mukaikaramon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''karamon'', and features two ''karahafu'' at the front and back of the gate. This type of gate may incorporate a ''karahafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon 向唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Hirakaramon===
[[File:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|upright|left|A ''hirakaramon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]
''Hirakaramon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''karahafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyukimon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Karayotsuashimon===
[[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|upright|left|''Yōmeimon'', a ''karayotsuashimon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Karayotsuashimon'' (唐四脚門, "Four-legged gate") is an ornate style of ''karamon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]].
{{Clear}}
==See also==
*[[Karahafu]] *[[Japanese architecture]] *[[Japanese Buddhist architecture]] *[[Japanese castle]] *[[Buddhist temples in Japan]] *[[Shinto shrine]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]]
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== {{refbegin}} *Coaldrake, William. (1996). ''Architecture and Authority in Japan''. London/New York: Routledge. {{ISBN|0-415-05754-X}}. *Sarvimaki Marja. (2000). ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings: Chinese and Korean Influence on Japanese Architecture''. Helsinki University of Technology, Department of Architecture. {{ISBN|0-521-36918-5}}. *Sarvimaki Marja. (2003). ''[http://sjeas.skku.edu/upload/200605/05_Sarvimaki%20Marja.pdf Layouts and Layers: Spatial Arrangements in Japan and Korea]''. Sungkyun Journal of East Asian Studies, Volume 3, No. 2. Retrieved on May 30, 2009. *Parent, Mary Neighbour. (2003). ''[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System]''. {{refend}}
{{commons category|Karamon}}
{{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Shinto shrine}}

JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref><ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." JAANUS. ''Karamon'' would later become a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80–108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''karamon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture.<ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>

This type of gate may incorporate a ''karahafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon 向唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Hirakaramon===
[[File:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|upright|left|A ''hirakaramon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]
''Hirakaramon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''karahafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyukimon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Karayotsuashimon===
[[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|upright|left|''Yōmeimon'', a ''karayotsuashimon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Karayotsuashimon'' (唐四脚門, "Four-legged gate") is an ornate style of ''karamon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]].
{{Clear}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kara-mon]][[en:Karamon]][[fr:Karamon]][[mr:कारामॉन]][[pt:Karamon]][[ru:Карамон]][[th:คารามง]][[zh:唐門 (日本)]] {{Short description|Type of gate found in Japanese architecture}} {{main|Mon (architecture)}} {{Italic title|reason=
}} [[File:Nijo Castle Karamon2.JPG|thumb|250px|A ''kara-mon'' at [[:en:Nijō Castle|Nijō Castle]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''kara-mon''''' or '''''kara-kado'''''|唐門}} is a type of [[:en:Gate|Gate]] seen in [[:en:Japanese architecture|Japanese architecture]]. It is characterized by the usage of ''[[:en:Kara-hafu|Kara-hafu]]'', an undulating [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] peculiar to Japan. ''Kara-mon'' are often used at the entrances of [[:en:Japanese castle|Japanese castle]]s, [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s, and have historically been a symbol of authority.
==History== {{further|Kara-hafu}} Although ''kara'' (唐) can be translated as meaning "China" or "[[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang]]", this type of roof with undulating bargeboards first appeared in Japan during the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu 唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' was associated by the Japanese with any type of ornate architecture regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and is shown in [[:en:Emakimono|picture scrolls]] to decorate gates, corridors, and [[:en:Palanquins|Palanquins]]. The oldest existing ''karahafu'' is found at [[Hōryū-ji]] temple.<ref name=JAANUS/>
Initially, the ''kara-hafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]], it became an important architectural element in the construction of a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''{{'}}s mansions and castles. The ''kara-mon'' entrance was reserved for the ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments. A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82–84, 178.</ref>
''Kara-mon'' would later become a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80–108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''kara-mon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture.<ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>
==Variations==
===Mukai-kara-mon===
[[File:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|upright|A ''Mukai-kara-mon'' at [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]
''Mukai-kara-mon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''kara-mon'', and features two ''kara-hafu'' at the front and back of the gate. This type of gate may incorporate a ''kara-hafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon 向唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Hira-kara-mon=== [[File:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|upright|A ''hira-kara-mon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]
''Hira-kara-mon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''kara-hafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyuki-mon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon=== [[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|upright|''Yōmeimon'', a ''kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] ''Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' (唐四脚門, "Four-legged gate") is an ornate style of ''kara-mon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. This ''kara-mon'' is decorated with 611 sculptures, including the [[Seven Lucky Gods]] and the [[:en:Eight Immortals|Eight Immortals]].<ref>[https://tabi-mag.jp/tg0218/ 日光東照宮・唐門]</ref>
{{Clear}}
==See also== *[[Mon (architecture)]] *[[:en:Japanese Buddhist architecture|Japanese Buddhist architecture]] *[[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] *[[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]]
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== {{refbegin}} *Coaldrake, William. (1996). ''Architecture and Authority in Japan''. London/New York: Routledge. {{ISBN|0-415-05754-X}}. *Sarvimaki Marja. (2000). ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings: Chinese and Korean Influence on Japanese Architecture''. Helsinki University of Technology, Department of Architecture. {{ISBN|0-521-36918-5}}. *Sarvimaki Marja. (2003). ''[http://sjeas.skku.edu/upload/200605/05_Sarvimaki%20Marja.pdf Layouts and Layers: Spatial Arrangements in Japan and Korea]''. Sungkyun Journal of East Asian Studies, Volume 3, No. 2. Retrieved on May 30, 2009. *Parent, Mary Neighbour. (2003). ''[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System]''. {{refend}}

''Kara-mon'' are often used at the entrances of [[:en:Japanese castle|Japanese castle]]s, [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s, and have historically been a symbol of authority.

JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' was associated by the Japanese with any type of ornate architecture regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and is shown in [[:en:Emakimono|picture scrolls]] to decorate gates, corridors, and [[:en:Palanquins|Palanquins]]. The oldest existing ''karahafu'' is found at [[Hōryū-ji]] temple.<ref name=JAANUS/>
Initially, the ''kara-hafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]], it became an important architectural element in the construction of a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''{{'}}s mansions and castles. The ''kara-mon'' entrance was reserved for the ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments. A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82–84, 178.</ref>
''Kara-mon'' would later become a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80–108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''kara-mon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture.<ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>
==Variations==
===Mukai-kara-mon===
[[File:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|upright|A ''Mukai-kara-mon'' at [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]
''Mukai-kara-mon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''kara-mon'', and features two ''kara-hafu'' at the front and back of the gate. This type of gate may incorporate a ''kara-hafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon 向唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Hira-kara-mon=== [[File:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|upright|A ''hira-kara-mon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]
''Hira-kara-mon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''kara-hafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyuki-mon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon=== [[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|upright|''Yōmeimon'', a ''kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] ''Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' (唐四脚門, "Four-legged gate") is an ornate style of ''kara-mon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. This ''kara-mon'' is decorated with 611 sculptures, including the [[Seven Lucky Gods]] and the [[:en:Eight Immortals|Eight Immortals]].<ref>[https://tabi-mag.jp/tg0218/ 日光東照宮・唐門]</ref>
{{Clear}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kara-mon]][[en:Karamon]][[fr:Karamon]][[mr:कारामॉन]][[pt:Karamon]][[ru:Карамон]][[th:คารามง]][[zh:唐門 (日本)]]

{{Italic title|reason=

}}
[[File:Nijo Castle Karamon2.JPG|thumb|250px|A ''kara-mon'' at [[:en:Nijō Castle|Nijō Castle]]]]
The {{nihongo|'''''kara-mon''''' or '''''kara-kado'''''|唐門}} is a type of [[:en:Gate|Gate]] seen in [[:en:Japanese architecture|Japanese architecture]].  It is characterized by the usage of ''[[:en:Kara-hafu|Kara-hafu]]'', an undulating [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] peculiar to Japan.  ''Kara-mon'' are often used at the entrances of [[:en:Japanese castle|Japanese castle]]s, [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s, and have historically been a symbol of authority.

==History==
{{further|Kara-hafu}}	
Although ''kara'' (唐) can be translated as meaning "China" or "[[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang]]", this type of roof with undulating bargeboards first appeared in Japan during the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu　唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' was associated by the Japanese with any type of ornate architecture regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon　唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and is shown in [[:en:Emakimono|picture scrolls]] to decorate gates, corridors, and [[:en:Palanquins|Palanquins]]. The oldest existing ''karahafu'' is found at [[Hōryū-ji]] temple.<ref name=JAANUS/>

Initially, the ''kara-hafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]], it became an important architectural element in the construction of a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''{{'}}s mansions and castles.  The ''kara-mon'' entrance was reserved for the ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments.  A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82–84, 178.</ref>

''Kara-mon'' would later become a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80–108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''kara-mon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture.<ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>

==Variations==

===Mukai-kara-mon===

[[File:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|upright|A ''Mukai-kara-mon'' at [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]

''Mukai-kara-mon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''kara-mon'', and features two ''kara-hafu'' at the front and back of the gate.  This type of gate may incorporate a ''kara-hafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon　向唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>

{{Clear}}

===Hira-kara-mon===
[[File:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|upright|A ''hira-kara-mon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]

''Hira-kara-mon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''kara-hafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate.  This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyuki-mon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon　平唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>

{{Clear}}

===Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon===
[[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|upright|''Yōmeimon'', a ''kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' (唐四脚門, "Four-legged gate") is an ornate style of ''kara-mon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. This ''kara-mon'' is decorated with 611 sculptures, including the [[Seven Lucky Gods]] and the [[:en:Eight Immortals|Eight Immortals]].<ref>[https://tabi-mag.jp/tg0218/ 日光東照宮・唐門]</ref>

{{Clear}}

==History== {{further|Kara-hafu}} Although ''kara'' (唐) can be translated as meaning "China" or "[[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang]]", this type of roof with undulating bargeboards first appeared in Japan during the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu 唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' was associated by the Japanese with any type of ornate architecture regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and is shown in [[:en:Emakimono|picture scrolls]] to decorate gates, corridors, and [[:en:Palanquins|Palanquins]]. The oldest existing ''karahafu'' is found at [[Hōryū-ji]] temple.<ref name=JAANUS/>
Initially, the ''kara-hafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]], it became an important architectural element in the construction of a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''{{'}}s mansions and castles. The ''kara-mon'' entrance was reserved for the ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments. A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82–84, 178.</ref>
''Kara-mon'' would later become a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80–108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''kara-mon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture.<ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>
==Variations==
===Mukai-kara-mon===
[[File:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|upright|A ''Mukai-kara-mon'' at [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]
''Mukai-kara-mon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''kara-mon'', and features two ''kara-hafu'' at the front and back of the gate. This type of gate may incorporate a ''kara-hafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon 向唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Hira-kara-mon=== [[File:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|upright|A ''hira-kara-mon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]
''Hira-kara-mon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''kara-hafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyuki-mon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon=== [[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|upright|''Yōmeimon'', a ''kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] ''Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' (唐四脚門, "Four-legged gate") is an ornate style of ''kara-mon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. This ''kara-mon'' is decorated with 611 sculptures, including the [[Seven Lucky Gods]] and the [[:en:Eight Immortals|Eight Immortals]].<ref>[https://tabi-mag.jp/tg0218/ 日光東照宮・唐門]</ref>
{{Clear}}

(2003). ''[http://sjeas.skku.edu/upload/200605/05_Sarvimaki%20Marja.pdf Layouts and Layers: Spatial Arrangements in Japan and Korea]''. Sungkyun Journal of East Asian Studies, Volume 3, No. 2. Retrieved on May 30, 2009. *Parent, Mary Neighbour. (2003). ''[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System]''. {{refend}}

}} [[File:Nijo Castle Karamon2.JPG|thumb|250px|A ''kara-mon'' at [[:en:Nijō Castle|Nijō Castle]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''kara-mon''''' or '''''kara-kado'''''|唐門}} is a type of [[:en:Gate|Gate]] seen in [[:en:Japanese architecture|Japanese architecture]]. It is characterized by the usage of ''[[:en:Kara-hafu|Kara-hafu]]'', an undulating [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] peculiar to Japan. ''Kara-mon'' are often used at the entrances of [[:en:Japanese castle|Japanese castle]]s, [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s, and have historically been a symbol of authority.
==History== {{further|Kara-hafu}} Although ''kara'' (唐) can be translated as meaning "China" or "[[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang]]", this type of roof with undulating bargeboards first appeared in Japan during the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu 唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' was associated by the Japanese with any type of ornate architecture regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and is shown in [[:en:Emakimono|picture scrolls]] to decorate gates, corridors, and [[:en:Palanquins|Palanquins]]. The oldest existing ''karahafu'' is found at [[Hōryū-ji]] temple.<ref name=JAANUS/>
Initially, the ''kara-hafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]], it became an important architectural element in the construction of a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''{{'}}s mansions and castles. The ''kara-mon'' entrance was reserved for the ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments. A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82–84, 178.</ref>
''Kara-mon'' would later become a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80–108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''kara-mon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture.<ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>
==Variations==
===Mukai-kara-mon===
[[File:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|upright|A ''Mukai-kara-mon'' at [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]

JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Hira-kara-mon=== [[File:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|upright|A ''hira-kara-mon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]
''Hira-kara-mon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''kara-hafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyuki-mon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon=== [[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|upright|''Yōmeimon'', a ''kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] ''Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' (唐四脚門, "Four-legged gate") is an ornate style of ''kara-mon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. This ''kara-mon'' is decorated with 611 sculptures, including the [[Seven Lucky Gods]] and the [[:en:Eight Immortals|Eight Immortals]].<ref>[https://tabi-mag.jp/tg0218/ 日光東照宮・唐門]</ref>
{{Clear}}
