[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Kara-mon}} [[fr:Karamon]] [[mr:कारामॉन]] [[pt:Karamon]] [[ru:Карамон]] [[th:คารามง]] [[zh:唐門 (日本)]]
{{wikidata link|Q3192940}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kara-mon]][[en:Karamon]][[fr:Karamon]][[mr:कारामॉन]][[pt:Karamon]][[ru:Карамон]][[th:คารามง]][[zh:唐門 (日本)]] {{Short description|Type of gate found in Japanese architecture}} {{main|Mon (architecture)}} {{Italic title|reason=
}} [[File:Nijo Castle Karamon2.JPG|thumb|250px|A ''kara-mon'' at [[:en:Nijō Castle|Nijō Castle]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''kara-mon''''' or '''''kara-kado'''''|唐門}} is a type of [[:en:Gate|Gate]] seen in [[:en:Japanese architecture|Japanese architecture]]. It is characterized by the usage of ''[[:en:Kara-hafu|Kara-hafu]]'', an undulating [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] peculiar to Japan. ''Kara-mon'' are often used at the entrances of [[:en:Japanese castle|Japanese castle]]s, [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s, and have historically been a symbol of authority.
==History== {{further|Kara-hafu}} Although ''kara'' (唐) can be translated as meaning "China" or "[[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang]]", this type of roof with undulating bargeboards first appeared in Japan during the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu 唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' was associated by the Japanese with any type of ornate architecture regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and is shown in [[:en:Emakimono|picture scrolls]] to decorate gates, corridors, and [[:en:Palanquins|Palanquins]]. The oldest existing ''karahafu'' is found at [[Hōryū-ji]] temple.<ref name=JAANUS/>
Initially, the ''kara-hafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]], it became an important architectural element in the construction of a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''{{'}}s mansions and castles. The ''kara-mon'' entrance was reserved for the ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments. A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82–84, 178.</ref>
''Kara-mon'' would later become a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80–108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''kara-mon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture.<ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>
==Variations==
===Mukai-kara-mon===
[[File:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|upright|A ''Mukai-kara-mon'' at [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]
''Mukai-kara-mon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''kara-mon'', and features two ''kara-hafu'' at the front and back of the gate. This type of gate may incorporate a ''kara-hafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon 向唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Hira-kara-mon=== [[File:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|upright|A ''hira-kara-mon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]
''Hira-kara-mon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''kara-hafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyuki-mon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon=== [[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|upright|''Yōmeimon'', a ''kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] ''Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' (唐四脚門, "Four-legged gate") is an ornate style of ''kara-mon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. This ''kara-mon'' is decorated with 611 sculptures, including the [[Seven Lucky Gods]] and the [[:en:Eight Immortals|Eight Immortals]].<ref>[https://tabi-mag.jp/tg0218/ 日光東照宮・唐門]</ref>
{{Clear}}
==See also== *[[Mon (architecture)]] *[[:en:Japanese Buddhist architecture|Japanese Buddhist architecture]] *[[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] *[[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]]
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== {{refbegin}} *Coaldrake, William. (1996). ''Architecture and Authority in Japan''. London/New York: Routledge. {{ISBN|0-415-05754-X}}. *Sarvimaki Marja. (2000). ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings: Chinese and Korean Influence on Japanese Architecture''. Helsinki University of Technology, Department of Architecture. {{ISBN|0-521-36918-5}}. *Sarvimaki Marja. (2003). ''[http://sjeas.skku.edu/upload/200605/05_Sarvimaki%20Marja.pdf Layouts and Layers: Spatial Arrangements in Japan and Korea]''. Sungkyun Journal of East Asian Studies, Volume 3, No. 2. Retrieved on May 30, 2009. *Parent, Mary Neighbour. (2003). ''[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System]''. {{refend}}

JAANUS. JAANUS. The oldest existing ''karahafu'' is found at [[Hōryū-ji]] temple.<ref name=JAANUS/>

===Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon=== [[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|upright|''Yōmeimon'', a ''kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] ''Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' (唐四脚門, "Four-legged gate") is an ornate style of ''kara-mon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. ===Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon=== [[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|upright|''Yōmeimon'', a ''kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] ''Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' (唐四脚門, "Four-legged gate") is an ornate style of ''kara-mon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. This ''kara-mon'' is decorated with 611 sculptures, including the [[Seven Lucky Gods]] and the [[:en:Eight Immortals|Eight Immortals]].<ref>[https://tabi-mag.jp/tg0218/ 日光東照宮・唐門]</ref>
{{Clear}}

(2003). ''[http://sjeas.skku.edu/upload/200605/05_Sarvimaki%20Marja.pdf Layouts and Layers: Spatial Arrangements in Japan and Korea]''. Sungkyun Journal of East Asian Studies, Volume 3, No. 2. [[en:Karamon]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Kara-mon}} [[fr:Karamon]] [[mr:कारामॉन]] [[pt:Karamon]] [[ru:Карамон]] [[th:คารามง]] [[zh:唐門 (日本)]]
{{wikidata link|Q3192940}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kara-mon]][[en:Karamon]][[fr:Karamon]][[mr:कारामॉन]][[pt:Karamon]][[ru:Карамон]][[th:คารามง]][[zh:唐門 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kara-mon]][[en:Karamon]][[fr:Karamon]][[mr:कारामॉन]][[pt:Karamon]][[ru:Карамон]][[th:คารามง]][[zh:唐門 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kara-mon]][[en:Karamon]][[fr:Karamon]][[mr:कारामॉन]][[pt:Karamon]][[ru:Карамон]][[th:คารามง]][[zh:唐門 (日本)]] {{Short description|Type of gate found in Japanese architecture}} {{main|Mon (architecture)}} {{Italic title|reason=
}} [[File:Nijo Castle Karamon2.JPG|thumb|250px|A ''kara-mon'' at [[:en:Nijō Castle|Nijō Castle]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''kara-mon''''' or '''''kara-kado'''''|唐門}} is a type of [[:en:Gate|Gate]] seen in [[:en:Japanese architecture|Japanese architecture]]. It is characterized by the usage of ''[[:en:Kara-hafu|Kara-hafu]]'', an undulating [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] peculiar to Japan. ''Kara-mon'' are often used at the entrances of [[:en:Japanese castle|Japanese castle]]s, [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s, and have historically been a symbol of authority.
==History== {{further|Kara-hafu}} Although ''kara'' (唐) can be translated as meaning "China" or "[[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang]]", this type of roof with undulating bargeboards first appeared in Japan during the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu 唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' was associated by the Japanese with any type of ornate architecture regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and is shown in [[:en:Emakimono|picture scrolls]] to decorate gates, corridors, and [[:en:Palanquins|Palanquins]]. The oldest existing ''karahafu'' is found at [[Hōryū-ji]] temple.<ref name=JAANUS/>
Initially, the ''kara-hafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]], it became an important architectural element in the construction of a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''{{'}}s mansions and castles. The ''kara-mon'' entrance was reserved for the ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments. A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82–84, 178.</ref>
''Kara-mon'' would later become a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80–108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''kara-mon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture.<ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>
==Variations==
===Mukai-kara-mon===
[[File:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|upright|A ''Mukai-kara-mon'' at [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]
''Mukai-kara-mon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''kara-mon'', and features two ''kara-hafu'' at the front and back of the gate. This type of gate may incorporate a ''kara-hafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon 向唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Hira-kara-mon=== [[File:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|upright|A ''hira-kara-mon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]
''Hira-kara-mon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''kara-hafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyuki-mon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon=== [[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|upright|''Yōmeimon'', a ''kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] ''Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' (唐四脚門, "Four-legged gate") is an ornate style of ''kara-mon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. This ''kara-mon'' is decorated with 611 sculptures, including the [[Seven Lucky Gods]] and the [[:en:Eight Immortals|Eight Immortals]].<ref>[https://tabi-mag.jp/tg0218/ 日光東照宮・唐門]</ref>
{{Clear}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kara-mon]][[en:Karamon]][[fr:Karamon]][[mr:कारामॉन]][[pt:Karamon]][[ru:Карамон]][[th:คารามง]][[zh:唐門 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kara-mon]][[en:Karamon]][[fr:Karamon]][[mr:कारामॉन]][[pt:Karamon]][[ru:Карамон]][[th:คารามง]][[zh:唐門 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kara-mon]][[en:Karamon]][[fr:Karamon]][[mr:कारामॉन]][[pt:Karamon]][[ru:Карамон]][[th:คารามง]][[zh:唐門 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kara-mon]][[en:Karamon]][[fr:Karamon]][[mr:कारामॉन]][[pt:Karamon]][[ru:Карамон]][[th:คารามง]][[zh:唐門 (日本)]] {{Short description|Type of gate found in Japanese architecture}} {{main|Mon (architecture)}} {{Italic title|reason=
}} [[File:Nijo Castle Karamon2.JPG|thumb|250px|A ''kara-mon'' at [[:en:Nijō Castle|Nijō Castle]]]] The {{nihongo|'''''kara-mon''''' or '''''kara-kado'''''|唐門}} is a type of [[:en:Gate|Gate]] seen in [[:en:Japanese architecture|Japanese architecture]]. It is characterized by the usage of ''[[:en:Kara-hafu|Kara-hafu]]'', an undulating [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] peculiar to Japan. ''Kara-mon'' are often used at the entrances of [[:en:Japanese castle|Japanese castle]]s, [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s, and have historically been a symbol of authority.
==History== {{further|Kara-hafu}} Although ''kara'' (唐) can be translated as meaning "China" or "[[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang]]", this type of roof with undulating bargeboards first appeared in Japan during the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu 唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' was associated by the Japanese with any type of ornate architecture regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and is shown in [[:en:Emakimono|picture scrolls]] to decorate gates, corridors, and [[:en:Palanquins|Palanquins]]. The oldest existing ''karahafu'' is found at [[Hōryū-ji]] temple.<ref name=JAANUS/>
Initially, the ''kara-hafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]], it became an important architectural element in the construction of a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''{{'}}s mansions and castles. The ''kara-mon'' entrance was reserved for the ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments. A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82–84, 178.</ref>
''Kara-mon'' would later become a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80–108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''kara-mon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture.<ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>
==Variations==
===Mukai-kara-mon===
[[File:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|upright|A ''Mukai-kara-mon'' at [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]
''Mukai-kara-mon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''kara-mon'', and features two ''kara-hafu'' at the front and back of the gate. This type of gate may incorporate a ''kara-hafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon 向唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Hira-kara-mon=== [[File:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|upright|A ''hira-kara-mon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]
''Hira-kara-mon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''kara-hafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyuki-mon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon=== [[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|upright|''Yōmeimon'', a ''kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] ''Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' (唐四脚門, "Four-legged gate") is an ornate style of ''kara-mon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. This ''kara-mon'' is decorated with 611 sculptures, including the [[Seven Lucky Gods]] and the [[:en:Eight Immortals|Eight Immortals]].<ref>[https://tabi-mag.jp/tg0218/ 日光東照宮・唐門]</ref>
{{Clear}}

Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' was associated by the Japanese with any type of ornate architecture regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon 唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and is shown in [[:en:Emakimono|picture scrolls]] to decorate gates, corridors, and [[:en:Palanquins|Palanquins]]. The oldest existing ''karahafu'' is found at [[Hōryū-ji]] temple.<ref name=JAANUS/>
Initially, the ''kara-hafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]], it became an important architectural element in the construction of a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''{{'}}s mansions and castles. The ''kara-mon'' entrance was reserved for the ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments. A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82–84, 178.</ref>
''Kara-mon'' would later become a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80–108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''kara-mon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture.<ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>
==Variations==
===Mukai-kara-mon===
[[File:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|upright|A ''Mukai-kara-mon'' at [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]
''Mukai-kara-mon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''kara-mon'', and features two ''kara-hafu'' at the front and back of the gate. This type of gate may incorporate a ''kara-hafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon 向唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Hira-kara-mon=== [[File:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|upright|A ''hira-kara-mon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]
''Hira-kara-mon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''kara-hafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate. This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyuki-mon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon 平唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>
{{Clear}}
===Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon=== [[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|upright|''Yōmeimon'', a ''kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] ''Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' (唐四脚門, "Four-legged gate") is an ornate style of ''kara-mon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. This ''kara-mon'' is decorated with 611 sculptures, including the [[Seven Lucky Gods]] and the [[:en:Eight Immortals|Eight Immortals]].<ref>[https://tabi-mag.jp/tg0218/ 日光東照宮・唐門]</ref>
{{Clear}}

{{Short description|Type of gate found in Japanese architecture}}
{{main|Mon (architecture)}}
{{Italic title|reason=

}}
[[File:Nijo Castle Karamon2.JPG|thumb|250px|A ''kara-mon'' at {{ill|Nijō Castle|en|Nijō Castle|WD=Q1013399|lt=Nijō Castle}}]]
The {{nihongo|'''''kara-mon''''' or '''''kara-kado'''''|唐門}} is a type of {{ill|Gate|en|Gate|WD=Q53060|lt=Gate}} seen in {{ill|Japanese architecture|en|Japanese architecture|WD=Q1422874|lt=Japanese architecture}}.  It is characterized by the usage of ''[[:en:Kara-hafu|Kara-hafu]]'', an undulating {{ill|Bargeboard|en|Bargeboard|WD=Q4881511|lt=Bargeboard}} peculiar to Japan.  ''Kara-mon'' are often used at the entrances of {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle}}s, {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples}} and {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s, and have historically been a symbol of authority.

==History==
{{further|Kara-hafu}}	
Although ''kara'' (唐) can be translated as meaning "China" or "{{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang}}", this type of roof with undulating bargeboards first appeared in Japan during the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu　唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' was associated by the Japanese with any type of ornate architecture regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon　唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} and is shown in {{ill|Emakimono|en|Emakimono|WD=Q1190781|lt=picture scrolls}} to decorate gates, corridors, and [[:en:Palanquins|Palanquins]]. The oldest existing ''karahafu'' is found at [[Hōryū-ji]] temple.<ref name=JAANUS/>

Initially, the ''kara-hafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}, it became an important architectural element in the construction of a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''{{'}}s mansions and castles.  The ''kara-mon'' entrance was reserved for the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments.  A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82–84, 178.</ref>

''Kara-mon'' would later become a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80–108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''kara-mon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture.<ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>

==Variations==

===Mukai-kara-mon===

[[File:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|upright|A ''Mukai-kara-mon'' at [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]

''Mukai-kara-mon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''kara-mon'', and features two ''kara-hafu'' at the front and back of the gate.  This type of gate may incorporate a ''kara-hafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon　向唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>

{{Clear}}

===Hira-kara-mon===
[[File:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|upright|A ''hira-kara-mon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]

''Hira-kara-mon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''kara-hafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate.  This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyuki-mon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon　平唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>

{{Clear}}

===Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon===
[[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|upright|''Yōmeimon'', a ''kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' (唐四脚門, "Four-legged gate") is an ornate style of ''kara-mon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}. This ''kara-mon'' is decorated with 611 sculptures, including the [[Seven Lucky Gods]] and the {{ill|Eight Immortals|en|Eight Immortals|WD=Q340843|lt=Eight Immortals}}.<ref>[https://tabi-mag.jp/tg0218/ 日光東照宮・唐門]</ref>

{{Clear}}

Although ''kara'' (唐) can be translated as meaning "China" or "{{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang}}", this type of roof with undulating bargeboards first appeared in Japan during the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu　唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' was associated by the Japanese with any type of ornate architecture regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon　唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} and is shown in {{ill|Emakimono|en|Emakimono|WD=Q1190781|lt=picture scrolls}} to decorate gates, corridors, and [[:en:Palanquins|Palanquins]]. The oldest existing ''karahafu'' is found at [[Hōryū-ji]] temple.<ref name=JAANUS/>

Initially, the ''kara-hafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}, it became an important architectural element in the construction of a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''{{'}}s mansions and castles.  The ''kara-mon'' entrance was reserved for the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments.  A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82–84, 178.</ref>

''Kara-mon'' would later become a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80–108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''kara-mon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture.<ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>

==Variations==

===Mukai-kara-mon===

[[File:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|upright|A ''Mukai-kara-mon'' at [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]

''Mukai-kara-mon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''kara-mon'', and features two ''kara-hafu'' at the front and back of the gate.  This type of gate may incorporate a ''kara-hafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon　向唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>

{{Clear}}

===Hira-kara-mon===
[[File:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|upright|A ''hira-kara-mon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]

''Hira-kara-mon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''kara-hafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate.  This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyuki-mon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon　平唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>

{{Clear}}

===Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon===
[[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|upright|''Yōmeimon'', a ''kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' (唐四脚門, "Four-legged gate") is an ornate style of ''kara-mon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}. This ''kara-mon'' is decorated with 611 sculptures, including the [[Seven Lucky Gods]] and the {{ill|Eight Immortals|en|Eight Immortals|WD=Q340843|lt=Eight Immortals}}.<ref>[https://tabi-mag.jp/tg0218/ 日光東照宮・唐門]</ref>

{{Clear}}

The {{nihongo|'''''kara-mon''''' or '''''kara-kado'''''|唐門}} is a type of {{ill|Gate|en|Gate|WD=Q53060|lt=Gate}} seen in {{ill|Japanese architecture|en|Japanese architecture|WD=Q1422874|lt=Japanese architecture}}.  It is characterized by the usage of ''[[:en:Kara-hafu|Kara-hafu]]'', an undulating {{ill|Bargeboard|en|Bargeboard|WD=Q4881511|lt=Bargeboard}} peculiar to Japan.  ''Kara-mon'' are often used at the entrances of {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle}}s, {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples}} and {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s, and have historically been a symbol of authority.

==History==
{{further|Kara-hafu}}	
Although ''kara'' (唐) can be translated as meaning "China" or "{{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang}}", this type of roof with undulating bargeboards first appeared in Japan during the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu　唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' was associated by the Japanese with any type of ornate architecture regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon　唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} and is shown in {{ill|Emakimono|en|Emakimono|WD=Q1190781|lt=picture scrolls}} to decorate gates, corridors, and [[:en:Palanquins|Palanquins]]. The oldest existing ''karahafu'' is found at [[Hōryū-ji]] temple.<ref name=JAANUS/>

Initially, the ''kara-hafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}, it became an important architectural element in the construction of a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''{{'}}s mansions and castles.  The ''kara-mon'' entrance was reserved for the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments.  A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82–84, 178.</ref>

''Kara-mon'' would later become a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80–108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''kara-mon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture.<ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>

===Mukai-kara-mon===

[[File:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|upright|A ''Mukai-kara-mon'' at [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]

JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>

{{Clear}}

Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>

[[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|upright|''Yōmeimon'', a ''kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' (唐四脚門, "Four-legged gate") is an ornate style of ''kara-mon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}. This ''kara-mon'' is decorated with 611 sculptures, including the [[Seven Lucky Gods]] and the {{ill|Eight Immortals|en|Eight Immortals|WD=Q340843|lt=Eight Immortals}}.<ref>[https://tabi-mag.jp/tg0218/ 日光東照宮・唐門]</ref>

The {{nihongo|'''''kara-mon''''' or '''''kara-kado'''''|唐門}} is a type of {{ill|Gate|en|Gate|WD=Q53060|lt=Gate}} seen in {{ill|Japanese architecture|en|Japanese architecture|WD=Q1422874|lt=Japanese architecture}}.  It is characterized by the usage of ''[[:en:Kara-hafu|Kara-hafu]]'', an undulating {{ill|Bargeboard|en|Bargeboard|WD=Q4881511|lt=Bargeboard}} peculiar to Japan.  ''Kara-mon'' are often used at the entrances of {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle}}s, {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples}} and {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s, and have historically been a symbol of authority.

==History==
{{further|Kara-hafu}}	
Although ''kara'' (唐) can be translated as meaning "China" or "{{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang}}", this type of roof with undulating bargeboards first appeared in Japan during the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu　唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' was associated by the Japanese with any type of ornate architecture regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon　唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} and is shown in {{ill|Emakimono|en|Emakimono|WD=Q1190781|lt=picture scrolls}} to decorate gates, corridors, and [[:en:Palanquins|Palanquins]]. The oldest existing ''karahafu'' is found at [[Hōryū-ji]] temple.<ref name=JAANUS/>

Initially, the ''kara-hafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}, it became an important architectural element in the construction of a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''{{'}}s mansions and castles.  The ''kara-mon'' entrance was reserved for the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments.  A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82–84, 178.</ref>

''Kara-mon'' would later become a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80–108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''kara-mon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture.<ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>

==Variations==

===Mukai-kara-mon===

[[File:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|upright|A ''Mukai-kara-mon'' at [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]

''Mukai-kara-mon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''kara-mon'', and features two ''kara-hafu'' at the front and back of the gate.  This type of gate may incorporate a ''kara-hafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon　向唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>

{{Clear}}

===Hira-kara-mon===
[[File:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|upright|A ''hira-kara-mon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]

''Hira-kara-mon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''kara-hafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate.  This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyuki-mon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon　平唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>

{{Clear}}

===Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon===
[[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|upright|''Yōmeimon'', a ''kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' (唐四脚門, "Four-legged gate") is an ornate style of ''kara-mon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}. This ''kara-mon'' is decorated with 611 sculptures, including the [[Seven Lucky Gods]] and the {{ill|Eight Immortals|en|Eight Immortals|WD=Q340843|lt=Eight Immortals}}.<ref>[https://tabi-mag.jp/tg0218/ 日光東照宮・唐門]</ref>

{{Clear}}

==See also==

*{{ill|Japanese Buddhist architecture|en|Japanese Buddhist architecture|WD=Q7883744|lt=Japanese Buddhist architecture}}
*{{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples in Japan}}

==Notes==
{{reflist}}

==References==
{{refbegin}}

*Sarvimaki Marja. (2000). ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings: Chinese and Korean Influence on Japanese Architecture''. Helsinki University of Technology, Department of Architecture. {{ISBN|0-521-36918-5}}.
*Sarvimaki Marja. (2003). ''[http://sjeas.skku.edu/upload/200605/05_Sarvimaki%20Marja.pdf Layouts and Layers: Spatial Arrangements in Japan and Korea]''. Sungkyun Journal of East Asian Studies, Volume 3, No. 2.  Retrieved on May 30, 2009.  
*Parent, Mary Neighbour. (2003).  ''[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System]''.  
{{refend}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kara-mon]][[en:Karamon]][[fr:Karamon]][[mr:कारामॉन]][[pt:Karamon]][[ru:Карамон]][[th:คารามง]][[zh:唐門 (日本)]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kara-mon]][[en:Karamon]][[fr:Karamon]][[mr:कारामॉन]][[pt:Karamon]][[ru:Карамон]][[th:คารามง]][[zh:唐門 (日本)]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kara-mon]][[en:Karamon]][[fr:Karamon]][[mr:कारामॉन]][[pt:Karamon]][[ru:Карамон]][[th:คารามง]][[zh:唐門 (日本)]]
{{Short description|Type of gate found in Japanese architecture}}
{{main|Mon (architecture)}}
{{Italic title|reason=

}}
[[File:Nijo Castle Karamon2.JPG|thumb|250px|A ''kara-mon'' at {{ill|Nijō Castle|en|Nijō Castle|WD=Q1013399|lt=Nijō Castle|1=Nijō Castle}}]]
The {{nihongo|'''''kara-mon''''' or '''''kara-kado'''''|唐門}} is a type of {{ill|Gate|en|Gate|WD=Q53060|lt=Gate|1=Gate}} seen in {{ill|Japanese architecture|en|Japanese architecture|WD=Q1422874|lt=Japanese architecture|1=Japanese architecture}}.  It is characterized by the usage of ''[[:en:Kara-hafu|Kara-hafu]]'', an undulating {{ill|Bargeboard|en|Bargeboard|WD=Q4881511|lt=Bargeboard|1=Bargeboard}} peculiar to Japan.  ''Kara-mon'' are often used at the entrances of {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle|1=Japanese castle}}s, {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} and {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}s, and have historically been a symbol of authority.

==History==
{{further|Kara-hafu}}	
Although ''kara'' (唐) can be translated as meaning "China" or "{{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang|1=Tang dynasty}}", this type of roof with undulating bargeboards first appeared in Japan during the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu　唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' was associated by the Japanese with any type of ornate architecture regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon　唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} and is shown in {{ill|Emakimono|en|Emakimono|WD=Q1190781|lt=picture scrolls|1=Emakimono}} to decorate gates, corridors, and [[:en:Palanquins|Palanquins]]. The oldest existing ''karahafu'' is found at [[Hōryū-ji]] temple.<ref name=JAANUS/>

Initially, the ''kara-hafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}, it became an important architectural element in the construction of a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''{{'}}s mansions and castles.  The ''kara-mon'' entrance was reserved for the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments.  A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82–84, 178.</ref>

''Kara-mon'' would later become a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80–108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''kara-mon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture.<ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>

==Variations==

===Mukai-kara-mon===

[[File:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|upright|A ''Mukai-kara-mon'' at [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]

''Mukai-kara-mon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''kara-mon'', and features two ''kara-hafu'' at the front and back of the gate.  This type of gate may incorporate a ''kara-hafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon　向唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>

{{Clear}}

===Hira-kara-mon===
[[File:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|upright|A ''hira-kara-mon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]

''Hira-kara-mon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''kara-hafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate.  This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyuki-mon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon　平唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>

{{Clear}}

===Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon===
[[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|upright|''Yōmeimon'', a ''kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' (唐四脚門, "Four-legged gate") is an ornate style of ''kara-mon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}. This ''kara-mon'' is decorated with 611 sculptures, including the [[Seven Lucky Gods]] and the {{ill|Eight Immortals|en|Eight Immortals|WD=Q340843|lt=Eight Immortals|1=Eight Immortals}}.<ref>[https://tabi-mag.jp/tg0218/ 日光東照宮・唐門]</ref>

{{Clear}}

*Parent, Mary Neighbour. (2003).  ''[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System]''.  
{{refend}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kara-mon]][[en:Karamon]][[fr:Karamon]][[mr:कारामॉन]][[pt:Karamon]][[ru:Карамон]][[th:คารามง]][[zh:唐門 (日本)]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kara-mon]][[en:Karamon]][[fr:Karamon]][[mr:कारामॉन]][[pt:Karamon]][[ru:Карамон]][[th:คารามง]][[zh:唐門 (日本)]]
{{Short description|Type of gate found in Japanese architecture}}
{{main|Mon (architecture)}}
{{Italic title|reason=

}}
[[File:Nijo Castle Karamon2.JPG|thumb|250px|A ''kara-mon'' at {{ill|Nijō Castle|en|Nijō Castle|qid=Q1013399|lt=Nijō Castle|1=Nijō Castle}}]]
The {{nihongo|'''''kara-mon''''' or '''''kara-kado'''''|唐門}} is a type of {{ill|Gate|en|Gate|qid=Q53060|lt=Gate|1=Gate}} seen in {{ill|Japanese architecture|en|Japanese architecture|qid=Q1422874|lt=Japanese architecture|1=Japanese architecture}}.  It is characterized by the usage of ''[[:en:Kara-hafu|Kara-hafu]]'', an undulating {{ill|Bargeboard|en|Bargeboard|qid=Q4881511|lt=Bargeboard|1=Bargeboard}} peculiar to Japan.  ''Kara-mon'' are often used at the entrances of {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|qid=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle|1=Japanese castle}}s, {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} and {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}s, and have historically been a symbol of authority.

==History==
{{further|Kara-hafu}}	
Although ''kara'' (唐) can be translated as meaning "China" or "{{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|qid=Q9683|lt=Tang|1=Tang dynasty}}", this type of roof with undulating bargeboards first appeared in Japan during the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm karahafu　唐破風]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> It was named thus because the word ''kara'' was associated by the Japanese with any type of ornate architecture regardless of origin.<ref name=JAANUS2>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm karamon　唐門]." JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref> The ''karahafu'' developed during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} and is shown in {{ill|Emakimono|en|Emakimono|qid=Q1190781|lt=picture scrolls|1=Emakimono}} to decorate gates, corridors, and [[:en:Palanquins|Palanquins]]. The oldest existing ''karahafu'' is found at [[Hōryū-ji]] temple.<ref name=JAANUS/>

Initially, the ''kara-hafu'' was used only in temples and aristocratic gateways, but starting from the beginning of the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|qid=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}, it became an important architectural element in the construction of a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]''{{'}}s mansions and castles.  The ''kara-mon'' entrance was reserved for the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|qid=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' during his ''onari'' visits to the retainer, or for the reception of the emperor at shogunate establishments.  A structure associated with these social connections naturally imparted special meaning.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Structures, Symbols and Meanings'' (2000), 18/2000, 82–84, 178.</ref>

''Kara-mon'' would later become a means to proclaim the prestige of a building and functioned as a symbol of both religious and secular architecture.<ref>Sarvimaki: ''Layouts and Layers'' (2003), Vol 3, No. 2, 80–108.</ref> In the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], the ''kara-mon'' gates were a powerful symbol of authority reflected in architecture.<ref>Coaldrake (1996), 197</ref>

==Variations==

===Mukai-kara-mon===

[[File:Kitano-tenmangu001.JPG|thumb|upright|A ''Mukai-kara-mon'' at [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]]]

''Mukai-kara-mon'' (向唐門) is the most common form of ''kara-mon'', and features two ''kara-hafu'' at the front and back of the gate.  This type of gate may incorporate a ''kara-hafu'' in the middle of the roof, or the entire gable itself may be a curved structure.<ref name=JAANUS3>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mukaikaramon.htm mukaikaramon　向唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>

{{Clear}}

===Hira-kara-mon===
[[File:DaigojiSanpoinKaramon.jpg|thumb|upright|A ''hira-kara-mon'' at [[Daigo-ji]]]]

''Hira-kara-mon'' (平唐門) are distinguished with two ''kara-hafu'' on the left and right sides of the gate.  This type of gate was originally used at palaces, and was once called ''miyuki-mon'' (御幸門).<ref name=JAANUS4>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hirakaramon.htm hirakaramon　平唐門]". JAANUS. Retrieved on June 12, 2009.</ref>

{{Clear}}

===Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon===
[[File:日光東照宮陽明門001.jpg|thumb|upright|''Yōmeimon'', a ''kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
''Kara-yotsu-ashi-mon'' (唐四脚門, "Four-legged gate") is an ornate style of ''kara-mon'' that features four undulating gables on all sides of the gate. A good example of this type of gate can be found at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qid=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}. This ''kara-mon'' is decorated with 611 sculptures, including the [[Seven Lucky Gods]] and the {{ill|Eight Immortals|en|Eight Immortals|qid=Q340843|lt=Eight Immortals|1=Eight Immortals}}.<ref>[https://tabi-mag.jp/tg0218/ 日光東照宮・唐門]</ref>

{{Clear}}
